prompt:
Ops flipped our CI runners to `python3 -W error` this quarter (every warning is now fatal) and the asset-tag scanner toolbox stopped passing the same day. The suite's first step force-recompiles the tree, and it prints this:

  *** Error compiling './scanlog.py'...
    File "./scanlog.py", line 15
      if kind is not "checkout" and kind is not "checkin":
         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  SyntaxError: "is not" with 'str' literal. Did you mean "!="?

  *** Error compiling './tagrules.py'...
    File "./tagrules.py", line 5
      TAG_PATTERN = re.compile("^(?P<site>[A-Z]{2})-(?P<num>\d{4})$")
                                                            ^^
  SyntaxError: invalid escape sequence '\d'

and I'd bet lunch those two lines aren't the only ones of their kind in there. To be clear about the goal: the scanner behavior itself was NEVER wrong in production — tag matching, the outstanding-tags wall list, the audit-date stamps all work — so this is a make-it-compile-clean job, not a rewrite. test_tagscan.py pins the behavior (including a couple of corner cases people get wrong when they touch regex strings) and the verify is exactly `python3 -W error test_tagscan.py`. Don't touch the test file; smallest possible diff please.
notes: Family-5C seed with 5W-style gating sanctioned by the catalog item: syntax-adjacent deprecations that fail compilation under -W error, behavior pinned. Planted: five invalid escape sequences across three non-raw regex pattern strings in tagrules.py (\d x3 occurrences within patterns, \s, \w, \. — all behavior-preserving, the fix is raw-stringing), and three `is`/`is not` with str literal comparisons in scanlog.py. Gate: test_tagscan.py's first step runs compileall.compile_dir(force=True) and asserts the result — force=True closes the stale-__pycache__ hole where a previously cached pyc would skip recompilation and hide the warnings; under -W error CPython surfaces escalated SyntaxWarnings as SyntaxError (probed on python 3.13.11). Teeth beyond the gate: the wildcard test pins that DATE_PATTERN dots stay literal (so stripping backslashes instead of raw-stringing fails), and parse_line classification is exercised with runtime-built, non-interned event words (so identity comparisons that dodge the warning still fail by value).
